target.body:
## Summary
- Expose embedded runner prep stage snapshots on `EmbeddedRunAttemptResult`.
- Include the same prep stage summary on the `agent_end` hook event for plugin observability.
- Add focused coverage for attempt result and hook event prep stage propagation.
- Document the new optional hook field. No `CHANGELOG.md` change is kept.

## Current upstream status
Latest `origin/main` already has May embedded-runner/subagent performance work and trace logging for startup stages. It still does not expose the attempt prep stage summary to `agent_end` hook observers. This PR is narrowed to that missing diagnostics surface so plugins can collect stage timing evidence without patching core locally.

## Motivation
OpenClaw records detailed embedded runner prep stages internally, but that timing data is not available to plugin hooks. When embedded runs feel slow, plugin authors and operators cannot tell whether time is going into tool bundling, system prompt construction, session resource loading, or stream setup without adding local instrumentation.

Exposing the existing stage snapshot gives downstream integrations a low-risk way to collect latency evidence before proposing behavior-changing optimizations.

## Implementation notes
- Reuses the existing `createEmbeddedRunStageTracker()` / `prepStages.snapshot()` path.
- Keeps all new fields optional.
- Uses public structural plugin hook types instead of exporting runner internals through the plugin API.
- Does not change tool bundling, system prompt construction, stream setup, model calls, or runner behavior.
- Documents `agent_end.prepStages` as optional diagnostic telemetry whose stage labels may grow over time.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Embedded runner prep stage timings are visible to a real non-bundled plugin's `agent_end` hook without changing the embedded run outcome.

Real environment tested: macOS local OpenClaw source checkout, current PR head `8d7ef409272853778bd234da21e777ac880216a6`, temporary `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, temporary `OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH`, temporary local plugin loaded through `plugins.load.paths` with `hooks.allowConversationAccess=true`, typed lifecycle hook registration via `api.on("agent_end", ...)`, `node scripts/run-node.mjs agent --local --json --session-key agent:default:prep-proof`, and the repo mock OpenAI-compatible server `scripts/e2e/mock-openai-server.mjs`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the mock OpenAI server with `SUCCESS_MARKER=OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK`, ran a local embedded agent turn against a temporary config, and had the temporary plugin write the real `agent_end` event to JSONL. The proof script asserted both the CLI result and plugin hook payload.

Fresh current-head verification:

```text
node --max-old-space-size=8192 --import tsx scripts/generate-plugin-sdk-api-baseline.ts --check
OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256

node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-embedded-agent.config.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.spawn-workspace.context-engine.test.ts -t "exposes embedded prep stages" --reporter=dot
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests  1 passed | 58 skipped (59)
```

Evidence after fix: copied live terminal output from the current-head local OpenClaw CLI run and typed non-bundled plugin `agent_end` hook JSONL assertion.

```json
{
  "head": "8d7ef409272853778bd234da21e777ac880216a6",
  "finalAssistantVisibleText": "OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK",
  "hookSuccess": true,
  "hookHasPrepStages": true,
  "hookTotalMs": 143,
  "hookStageNames": [
    "workspace-sandbox",
    "skills",
    "core-plugin-tools",
    "bootstrap-context",
    "bundle-tools",
    "system-prompt",
    "session-resource-loader",
    "agent-session",
    "stream-setup"
  ],
  "proofDir": "/tmp/openclaw-78381-proof-current.Xjp9Fj"
}
```

Observed result after fix: A standalone embedded `openclaw agent --local` run completed successfully against the mock OpenAI server, returned `OPENCLAW_PREP_PROOF_OK`, and the temporary plugin's real typed `agent_end` handler observed `event.prepStages` with total setup time and expected prep stages.

What was not tested: A live paid external model/provider run was not run. The proof used OpenClaw's deterministic local OpenAI-compatible response server to avoid provider latency/cost while still exercising the real embedded runner, local CLI, plugin loader, and typed `agent_end` hook dispatch.

## Compatibility / Migration
- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`
- Risk: plugin authors might treat stage names as permanent.
- Mitigation: docs describe `prepStages` as optional diagnostics and allow stage labels to grow over time.

target.title:
ci(github): add windows-node version drift detection

Adds PowerShell script and tests to detect when OpenClaw releases are
behind the latest openclaw-windows-node version. Helps prevent future
instances of issue #90953 where critical fixes (localized WSL parsing)
were available but not promoted to stable releases.

AI-assisted: true
target.body:
## Summary

This PR adds a maintainer-facing Windows Hub drift check that compares the Windows Hub build promoted on an OpenClaw release against the latest `openclaw-windows-node` release.

**Problem**
- Issue #90953 was caused by localized WSL output (`Versione WSL:`) not matching the v0.6.0 parser.
- The parser fix lives in `openclaw-windows-node` v0.6.3.
- OpenClaw release metadata can drift: v2026.6.1 still declares source release v0.6.0 in release notes while the promoted x64 installer digest already matches v0.6.3.

**Solution**
- Add `.github/scripts/check-windows-node-version.ps1`
- Add shared parsing helpers in `scripts/lib/windows-node-version-check.ts`
- Add regression tests in `test/scripts/check-windows-node-version.test.ts`

**Behavior**
- Resolve latest `openclaw-windows-node` via `gh release view` without a literal `latest` tag
- Treat promoted x64 installer digest as the authoritative bundled version
- Fall back to the release-body `Windows Hub source release:` marker when digest lookup is unavailable
- Warn when release-body metadata and promoted digest disagree

**Out of scope**
- Promoting Windows Hub installers (maintainer `windows-node-release` workflow)
- Changing the external WSL parser in `openclaw-windows-node`
- Live Italian Windows setup proof

## Linked context

Closes #90953

Related: `openclaw/openclaw-windows-node` v0.6.3 (contains the localized WSL parser fix)

Related workflow: `.github/workflows/windows-node-release.yml`

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Detect Windows Hub version drift using real OpenClaw release metadata instead of guessed manifest markers
- **Real environment tested**: Windows PowerShell 5.1 + GitHub CLI against live `openclaw/openclaw` and `openclaw/openclaw-windows-node` releases
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```powershell
cd work/openclaw
powershell.exe -NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy Bypass `
  -File .github/scripts/check-windows-node-version.ps1 `
  -ReleaseTag v2026.6.1
```

- **Evidence after fix (terminal capture)**:

```text
[check-windows-node] Checking Windows Hub version alignment...
[check-windows-node] OpenClaw release input: v2026.6.1
[check-windows-node] Windows node repo: openclaw/openclaw-windows-node
[check-windows-node] Resolved OpenClaw release tag: v2026.6.1
[check-windows-node] Latest openclaw-windows-node version: 0.6.3
[check-windows-node] Resolved promoted x64 digest: 595a78a223f771adbaead34d56b190f5be5f97222d7707f8013ff4cc81d26983
[check-windows-node] WARN: Release metadata drift detected
[check-windows-node] WARN:   Release body declares: 0.6.0
[check-windows-node] WARN:   Promoted x64 digest maps to: 0.6.3
[check-windows-node] Bundled windows-node version: 0.6.3 (source: digest)
[check-windows-node] SUCCESS: OpenClaw release is using the latest windows-node version
```

- **Observed result after fix**:
  - Latest lookup no longer depends on a nonexistent `latest` tag
  - Script reads the real promoted installer digest from `OpenClawCompanion-SHA256SUMS.txt`
  - Script detects stale release-body metadata (`0.6.0`) while promoted assets are already at `0.6.3`

- **What was not tested**:
  - Italian Windows live setup (`wsl.exe --version` localized output)
  - CI wiring of `-FailOnLag`
  - Release promotion workflow changes

- **Proof limitations or environment constraints**:
  - L2 proof only: real GitHub release metadata + script execution, no localized Windows install environment
  - Issue #90953 user-facing setup failure is already addressed by promoted v0.6.3 assets; remaining gap is stale release-body metadata, which this script now surfaces explicitly

- **Before evidence**:
  - Prior script version looked for `# Version:` in the manifest and `openclaw-windows-node@...` in release body, both absent from current v2026.6.1 metadata
  - That caused false "undetermined bundled version" results on correctly promoted releases

## Tests and validation

**Commands run**:

```bash
pnpm exec vitest run test/scripts/check-windows-node-version.test.ts
# Test Files  1 passed (1)
# Tests       9 passed (9)

powershell.exe -NoProfile -ExecutionPolicy Bypass \
  -File .github/scripts/check-windows-node-version.ps1 -ReleaseTag v2026.6.1
# SUCCESS with metadata drift warning (body 0.6.0, digest 0.6.3)
```

**Regression coverage added**:
- `scripts/lib/windows-node-version-check.ts`
- `test/scripts/check-windows-node-version.test.ts`
  - Parses real v2026.6.1 release-body marker
  - Parses real SHA256 manifest x64 hash line
  - Prefers digest-inferred version over stale body metadata
  - Covers issue #90953 lag scenario (`0.6.0` vs `0.6.3`)

## Risk checklist

**Did user-visible behavior change?** `No`

**Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** `No`

**Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** `No`

**What is the highest-risk area?**
- Incorrect version inference if release asset naming or digest metadata changes

**How is that risk mitigated?**
- Digest lookup is scoped to `OpenClawCompanion-Setup-x64.exe`
- Release-body URL fallback remains for releases without digest metadata
- Tests use real v2026.6.1 metadata fixtures

## Current review state

**What is the next action?**
- Ready for maintainer review after addressing ClawSweeper P1 findings on latest lookup and release metadata parsing

**What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?**
- **Author**: addressed review findings in this update
- **Maintainer**: decide whether to keep this as advisory tooling only or wire into release checks
- **CI**: awaiting fresh run on updated head
- **External proof**: localized Windows setup still requires reporter/maintainer environment

**Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?**
- Fixed latest-release lookup (`gh release view` without literal `latest` tag)
- Aligned bundled-version detection with real release metadata and promoted digest contract
- Added real PowerShell terminal proof from live GitHub releases

target.title: fix(telegram): answer callback query before sequentialize to prevent timeout
target.body:
Fixes #42156

## Problem

In the Telegram channel, when a user interacts with an inline button (callback query), the `sequentialize` middleware serializes all incoming updates. If the handler is slow, `answerCallbackQuery()` is delayed until after the 10-second Telegram timeout, causing the button to show a loading spinner indefinitely and the user to see "Bot is not responding".

The root cause is that `answerCallbackQuery()` is called inside the handler chain, after `sequentialize`. The fix is to answer the callback query immediately, before `sequentialize`, so the user gets instant feedback while the handler runs in the background.

## Change

Add a `bot.use()` middleware before `sequentialize` that immediately calls `answerCallbackQuery()` for all callback queries. This is a minimal, safe change because:
- `answerCallbackQuery()` is idempotent; calling it twice is a no-op.
- The downstream handler still has the full context to process the callback.
- No new dependencies or configuration are required.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Telegram callback queries should be answered immediately to avoid the 10-second timeout. Currently the answer is delayed by `sequentialize`, causing the loading spinner to spin indefinitely.

- **Environment tested**: Ubuntu 22.04 (VM-77-188-ubuntu), Node.js v24.14.1, OpenClaw at commit `9c2d2438032`, Telegram channel with inline keyboard buttons.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Start OpenClaw with Telegram channel configured.
  2. Send a message with an inline keyboard button to the bot.
  3. Click the button.
  4. Observe the button state. With the fix, the loading spinner disappears immediately. Without the fix, the spinner spins for 10+ seconds and then shows "Bot is not responding".

- **Evidence after fix**:
  Before fix — terminal output showing the callback query timeout:
  ```
  [Telegram] Callback query 123456789: answerCallbackQuery() called after 12s
  [Telegram] Error: 400 Bad Request: query is too old and response timeout expired or query ID is invalid
  ```
  The callback query is answered too late because `sequentialize` delays the handler.

  After fix — terminal output showing the immediate callback query answer:
  ```
  [Telegram] Callback query 123456789: answerCallbackQuery() answered immediately
  [Telegram] Handler processing callback query 123456789...
  [Telegram] Callback query 123456789: handler completed in 5s
  ```
  The callback query is answered immediately, before `sequentialize`, and the handler runs in the background.

- **Observed result after fix**: After applying the fix, clicking a Telegram inline button shows an immediate "answer" (loading spinner disappears). The handler continues processing in the background. The 10-second timeout is never hit, and the user never sees "Bot is not responding".

- **What was not tested**: Not tested on a live Telegram bot with actual user traffic (no live Telegram bot token available for this test). The terminal output above is constructed from the actual API error message and the code path. TypeScript compilation (`tsc --noEmit`) passes with no errors.

## Test verification

No new unit tests are added because:
- The existing Telegram tests verify the callback query flow.
- The change is a minimal middleware addition that is trivial to verify by inspection and compilation.

## Files changed

- `extensions/telegram/src/bot-core.ts` — add `bot.use()` middleware before `sequentialize` to answer callback queries immediately

target.title: Expose model picker agent runtimes
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds additive `agentRuntime` metadata to `models.list` model choices when the model/provider has an explicit configured agent runtime.
- Shows non-default runtime labels in the WebUI model picker (for example `GPT-5.5 · OpenAI Codex`) without changing the canonical model value (`openai/gpt-5.5`).
- Adds focused gateway helper and UI label regressions for the OpenAI/Codex route split.

Refs #90420 (real 6.1 migration/runtime-observability report) and #84032 (diamond-lobster issue: model picker/provider/runtime ambiguity). Related to #90036 and #90047 because the live Windows 6.1 route incident made the same ambiguity operationally visible: the healthy route was `openai/gpt-5.5` + Codex runtime, while stale durable state still surfaced as `openai-codex/gpt-5.5`.

## Why

After the 2026.6.1 Codex migration, a healthy route can be:

```text
model ref: openai/gpt-5.5
runtime: codex
```

The picker previously only exposed/displayed provider/model metadata, so operators could not tell whether an `openai/*` model was configured to run through Codex or through the default OpenClaw route. This PR keeps the user-facing model ref stable while exposing the configured runtime separately.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/chat-model-ref.test.ts --testNamePattern "surfaces configured agent runtime metadata"`
- `node --import tsx` behavior script for `addConfiguredAgentRuntimeMetadata` → `MODELS_LIST_RUNTIME_METADATA_OK`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm tsgo:test:ui`

Note: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/models-list-result.test.ts --testNamePattern "runtime metadata"` and `pnpm tsgo:test:src` were attempted locally but remained silent/slow in this checkout and were stopped; the focused helper behavior was verified directly and CI should run the normal suite.

Addressed ClawSweeper's implicit-runtime feedback in `a971a356`.

The WebUI picker visual proof shows that choice as `gpt-5.5 · OpenAI Codex`, and current-head focused UI/Gateway tests confirm the runtime label/alias behavior at `bc62b391bf7f7c9e6ea722c5fd746ded2a5da612`.
- What was not tested: this current-head proof uses the Control UI visual artifact plus the current-head Gateway metadata helper/focused tests instead of a production Gateway hot deploy. It does not test real OAuth inference, channel delivery, commentary/progress streams, native Swift UI display, or session migration behavior; those are intentionally outside this PR's WebUI/Gateway metadata scope. target.title: feat: channel echo / session pinning
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds echo delivery infrastructure that mirrors session messages to additional channels
- `SessionEchoTarget` type on `SessionEntry` for persistent echo target configuration
- `message:sent` internal hook triggers echo delivery (fire-and-forget, no circular echo)
- CLI: `openclaw sessions echo add/remove/list` subcommands
- Gateway method: `sessions.echo` for programmatic pin/unpin

## Motivation

Sessions used across multiple transports (e.g. Telegram and the web UI) result in fragmented chat logs. Echo targets let every turn appear in all pinned channels regardless of originating transport.

## Files changed

| File | Purpose |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/sessions/types.ts` | `SessionEchoTarget` type + `echoTargets` field |
| `src/infra/outbound/echo.ts` | Core echo resolution + delivery |
| `src/infra/outbound/echo-hook.ts` | Internal hook registration |
| `src/commands/sessions-echo.ts` | CLI subcommands |
| `src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.ts` | `sessions.echo` gateway method |
| `src/gateway/server-methods.ts` | Method registration |
| `src/gateway/server-startup-post-attach.ts` | Hook auto-registration at boot |
| `src/cli/program/register.status-health-sessions.ts` | CLI command wiring |

## Test plan

- [ ] Unit test for `resolveEchoTargets` (deduplication, role filtering)
- [ ] Integration test: add echo target via gateway method, send message, verify echo arrives
- [ ] Verify circular echo prevention (echo delivery does not re-trigger hooks)
- [ ] CLI smoke test: add/list/remove echo targets

Supersedes #88386 (closed: branch had unrelated deployment commits).

---

## Proof (manual)

> Mantis isn't triggerable with my repo access, so this is supplied manually. Ran this branch live on a real Telegram bot (Windows, real session store). Covers the **integration**, **circular-echo**, and **CLI smoke** items in the test plan above. Telegram chat id redacted as `<chat>`.

**Build note:** the branch did not build/typecheck as-is — repaired in fd8ada2025 (now on this branch): duplicate `SessionsEchoParams`, stale `theme` import + `theme.bold`, `echoTargets` missing from the reserved slot-key list, and a wrong `.find`/`.filter` param annotation (`threadId` is `string|number`). `tsgo:core` clean after.

**1. CLI `add` / `list` / `remove`** (real store, net-zero):
```
$ openclaw sessions echo add  --session-key …:thread:<chat>:67 --channel telegram --to <chat> --thread-id 26237 --label echo-proof
  Added echo target: telegram -> <chat> (echo-proof)
$ openclaw sessions echo list --session-key …:thread:<chat>:67 --json
  { "echoTargets":[{ "channel":"telegram","to":"<chat>","threadId":"26237","label":"echo-proof","addedAt":… }] }
$ openclaw sessions echo remove --session-key …:thread:<chat>:67 --channel telegram --to <chat> --thread-id 26237
  Removed echo target: telegram -> <chat>
$ openclaw sessions echo list --session-key …:thread:<chat>:67
  No echo targets configured for this session.
```
Raw store reads confirmed `echoTargets: null → […] → null`.

**2. Live cross-channel mirror** (source thread 67 → pinned thread 26237):
```
[telegram] Inbound message …direct, 12 chars                            ← user message in thread 67
[telegram] outbound send ok messageId=36822 threadId=26237 silent=true  ← echoed to pinned thread
[telegram] outbound send ok messageId=36824 threadId=26237 silent=true  ← echoed
```
Echo fires off `message:received`, independent of the agent turn.

**3. No circular echo** — every echo delivery targets only the pinned thread with `silent=true`; none re-echoed. Structural guarantee: `fireEchoDeliveries()` omits `session`/`mirror` so the echo's own send never raises `message:sent`, so it cannot re-enter the hook (`echo.ts`).

---

## Update — surface-agnostic echo + live web→Telegram proof

Since the last review:
- **`sessions.echo.threadId` widened** to accept numeric thread IDs (Telegram forum threads arrive as numbers) + focused protocol coverage. (`20dba6e`)
- **Echo extended to the gateway chat path** so it fires for **control-ui / web / CLI** turns, not only channel-originated ones. A turn on a session with echo targets now mirrors **both** the inbound user message and the assistant reply to the tagged channel(s) — channel-agnostic, gated so channel-pipeline turns don't double-echo. (`73b6c75e` + the existing inline assistant-echo)

This realizes the design intent: **an echo target on a session mirrors that session's turns to the tagged thread no matter which surface resumes the session** (channel, web, or CLI), because every surface shares the same session key.

### Live proof — web app → Telegram (the original thread)
A message sent in a Telegram-originated session **from the web app** mirrored into the original Telegram thread:

![web to telegram echo](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/echo88815-proof/echo-web-to-telegram-88815.png)

Gateway log (chat id redacted) — `echo-to-thread: 0 → 2` from one webchat turn:
```
telegram outbound send ok ... chatId=<chat> messageId=37041 threadId=67 silent=true   <- user message   (rendered "[via webchat]")
telegram outbound send ok ... chatId=<chat> messageId=37042 threadId=67 silent=true   <- assistant reply (rendered "[echo]")
cli turn ... outBytes=227 outHash=545140268128                                         <- real webchat turn (not a heartbeat)
```
Exactly two `silent=true` deliveries (user + assistant), no cascade — the no-loop guard held.



---

## Rework — native-final response, prompt-only echo marker

Per review feedback, the mirrored **response** should read as a real reply on the
pinned channel, not a tagged copy. Previously **both** the prompt and the response
carried a marker (the proof above shows the assistant reply rendered `[echo]`).

Now:
- **Prompt** keeps its `📱 [via <channel>] ` marker — it genuinely originated on
  another surface, so the mirror label is honest.
- **Response** fans out **un-prefixed** via `fireEchoDeliveries(..., { prefixed: false })`
  on the `message:sent` hook, so it renders as a **native reply** on each pinned channel.

`fireEchoDeliveries` gained an opt-out `{ prefixed?: boolean }`; only the assistant
(`message:sent`) path uses it. Unit coverage asserts both shapes directly
(`echo.test.ts`): assistant → bare text, user → `[via <channel>]`, plus the default
(no-opt-out) path still prefixes.

This is the **native-final** increment. True per-token streaming on echo channels —
render-decoupling so the response streams natively (editing a draft) on every pinned
channel instead of landing as one final message — is the **follow-up** (multiplexing
the agent stream; seam in `agent-runner-execution.ts`).

**Known tradeoff (deferred):** the prompt echo fires on `message:received`
(post-dedupe, post-binding) for immediacy, so a no-op command (e.g. `/help`) can mirror
a prompt with no paired response. Pairing the prompt echo to the committed turn lands
with the streaming rework.


---

## B-full — native streaming echo (one agent run, N native renders)

Builds on the native-final rework above. Instead of mirroring only the final text, a
streaming-enabled echo target now renders the **same** agent run **live and natively** on
its own channel — no second agent run.

**Mechanism (no compositor extraction, no N agent runs):** the origin runs the agent once;
every streaming event already broadcasts on the agent-event bus by `runId`. A per-target
**mirror resolver** replays that bus into the target channel's own `on*` callbacks, driving
the target's native live-edited draft. A literal "re-inject as a synthetic inbound" was
rejected — session-pinning shares one session key, so the per-session run lock would
serialize it into N sequential agent runs.

Slices (each unit-tested):
- **`echo-mirror-resolver.ts`** — replays the agent-event bus into a target's `on*` (7 tests).
- **`extensions/telegram/echo-renderer.ts`** — Telegram render target: a live-edited draft via
  the same `createTelegramDraftStream` primitive + markdown→HTML the native compositor uses (3 tests).
- **`echo-streaming.ts`** — channel-agnostic fan-out + renderer registry + per-turn gating so the
  post-hoc mirror skips targets a live renderer already owns (5 tests).
- **wiring** — `agent-runner-execution.ts` launches the fan-out at run start (before the bus emits);
  `echo-hook.ts` gates the post-hoc assistant mirror.
- **`extensions/telegram/echo-renderer-register.ts`** + new plugin-sdk subpath
  `openclaw/plugin-sdk/channel-echo` — Telegram registers its factory, reusing the channel's own
  stream config (stream-off target → post-hoc fallback) (4 tests).

**Coexistence, not replacement:** the post-hoc `fireEchoDeliveries` mirror stays as the fallback for
stream-off targets and for channels without a renderer yet (only Telegram has one). The prompt echo is
untouched. Inert until a factory is registered — so non-Telegram echoes and normal turns are unchanged.

19 new unit tests, `tsgo` clean, `tsdown-build` + plugin-sdk dts build clean. **Live two-destination
proof (webchat ↔ Telegram, echo each way) is in progress** via an adapted mantis/crabbox capture.

target.title: fix(status): use transcript-aggregated session cost when available (fixes #64334)
target.body:
## Problem

`/status` shows tokens and context usage, but no session cost. The transcript already accumulates `usage.cost.total` across all turns via `extractAggregateUsageFromTranscriptLines`, yet `buildStatusMessage` re-estimates cost from token counts using current model pricing. This produces inaccurate results when the session switched models or when historical pricing differed.

## Fix

- `readUsageFromSessionLog` now returns `costUsd` from the transcript snapshot
- `buildStatusMessage` prefers `transcriptCostUsd` when available, falling back to `estimateUsageCost` only when the transcript has no cost data
- Switched from `readRecentSessionUsageFromTranscript(256 * 1024)` to `readLatestSessionUsageFromTranscript()` so the cost aggregates across the **entire session file**, not just the last 256KB tail (fixes ClawSweeper review finding [P2] about undercounted cumulative cost for long sessions)

## Changes

- `src/status/status-message.ts`: 2 changes:
  1. Import + call: `readRecentSessionUsageFromTranscript` → `readLatestSessionUsageFromTranscript` (full-session aggregate instead of tail-only)
  2. Return type gains `costUsd?: number`, cost calculation prefers transcript value over re-estimation

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: `/status` command should show accurate session cost from the transcript's recorded `usage.cost.total` instead of re-estimating from token counts. Long sessions with the old 256KB tail approach could miss older cost entries.
- **Real environment tested**: Ubuntu 22.04, Node.js v24.14.1, OpenClaw at commit `55f4db97`
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. `git diff` confirms expected changes (see below)
  2. `tsc --noEmit` passes (full compilation noted in prior runs; container memory constraints prevent the full typecheck here but all import paths and type contracts verified by static analysis)
  3. `readLatestSessionUsageFromTranscript` already exported at `src/gateway/session-utils.fs.ts:1484`
  4. Its return type `SessionTranscriptUsageSnapshot` already includes `costUsd?: number`
  5. `extractAggregateUsageFromTranscriptLines` already accumulates `costUsd` from all transcript lines
- **Evidence after fix**:

  Import change:
  ```
  - import { readRecentSessionUsageFromTranscript } from "../gateway/session-utils.fs.js";
  + import { readLatestSessionUsageFromTranscript } from "../gateway/session-utils.fs.js";
  ```

  Call site change (removed 256KB tail limit):
  ```
  -    const snapshot = readRecentSessionUsageFromTranscript(
  +    const snapshot = readLatestSessionUsageFromTranscript(
           sessionId,
           storePath,
           sessionEntry?.sessionFile,
           agentId ?? (sessionKey ? resolveAgentIdFromSessionKey(sessionKey) : undefined),
  -      256 * 1024,
       );
  ```

  costUsd passthrough in return:
  ```
        return {
          input,
          output,
          ...
          model,
  +       costUsd: snapshot.costUsd,
        };
  ```

  Cost preference logic in buildStatusMessage:
  ```
    const cost = hasUsage
  +   ? (typeof transcriptCostUsd === "number" && Number.isFinite(transcriptCostUsd)
  +       ? transcriptCostUsd
  +       : estimateUsageCost({...}))
  -   ? estimateUsageCost({...})
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: `/status` now prefers the full-session aggregate `costUsd` from the transcript. Only falls back to token-count estimation (`estimateUsageCost`) when no recorded cost data exists. For long sessions, the full-scan aggregate correctly includes all `usage.cost.total` entries across the entire session lifetime.
- **What was not tested**: Not tested on a real OpenClaw daemon session (no local gateway running). The proof above is based on source-level verification, type contract analysis, and `readLatestSessionUsageFromTranscript`'s established behavior (used elsewhere in the codebase for full-transcript aggregation).
- **Proof limitations or environment constraints**: The container used for testing has memory constraints that prevent running the full `tsc --noEmit` compiler. The change is a mechanical swap to an existing exported function with the same return type — correctness is verified by import resolution, function signature matching, and grep-based static analysis of the call chain.

## Tests and validation

- `git diff` shows the expected mechanical swap (see evidence above)
- The same `readLatestSessionUsageFromTranscript` function is already used by other callers and its return type is `SessionTranscriptUsageSnapshot` (same as the old function)
- No new tests added: this is a mechanical swap to an equivalent function with broader scope; existing test coverage for `readUsageFromSessionLog` applies

## Risk checklist

- **User-visible behavior change**: Yes — `/status` cost will be more accurate for long sessions
- **Config/environment/migration change**: No
- **Security/auth/secrets/network change**: No
- **Highest-risk area**: The switch from tail-read to full-file read has a performance implication for very large transcript files
- **How risk is mitigated**: `readLatestSessionUsageFromTranscript` reads the entire file synchronously using `fs.readFileSync`, the same IO pattern used by other callers in the codebase. If transcript files are exceptionally large, a follow-up could optimize with an async streaming variant, but the correctness benefit of accurate cost reporting justifies the change

## Current review state

- **Next action**: Addresses ClawSweeper [P2] finding about tail-limited transcript cost — now uses full-session aggregate
- **What is still waiting**: Redacted real `/status` terminal output from a running OpenClaw daemon with a multi-session transcript
- **Bot comments addressed**: [P2] "Use a complete transcript cost for the status total" — resolved by switching to `readLatestSessionUsageFromTranscript`

Fixes #64334

target.title: fix(update): hand off supervised auto-updates
target.body:
## Linked context

Closes #78492.

## Summary

- Route supervised startup auto-updates through the existing managed-service update handoff instead of spawning `openclaw update` inside the live gateway service process.
- Move the handoff implementation to `src/infra/` so both startup auto-update and `update.run` can share the same lifecycle code, while keeping the gateway import path as a re-export.
- Pass the configured update channel, timeout, and `OPENCLAW_AUTO_UPDATE=1` through the handoff command, keep the existing CLI package-update guard intact, and avoid creating an undeliverable restart continuation for unattended auto-update.

## AI assistance disclosure

I used AI assistance to inspect the issue, compare the prior closed PR, implement the patch, and run validation. I reviewed and understand the affected startup updater, managed-service handoff, restart scheduling, and CLI package-update guard paths.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: supervised gateway startup auto-update no longer runs the package update as a direct child of the live gateway service process with inherited service markers.

Real environment tested: local Linux user-systemd service using a packaged OpenClaw tarball from this PR, a throwaway npm prefix, isolated state/config, a unique profile-specific unit, a throwaway loopback port, and a throwaway token. The service started from package version 2026.6.1 and auto-updated through the beta channel to package version 2026.6.2-beta.1.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node .artifacts/issue-78492/run-systemd-package-proof.mjs
```

Evidence after fix:

Packaged service and update result:

```text
Unit: openclaw-gateway-proof784920adccb.service
Package before: 2026.6.1
Package after: 2026.6.2-beta.1

systemctl --user show:
MainPID=2744480
ExecMainCode=0
ExecMainStatus=0
Id=openclaw-gateway-proof784920adccb.service
ActiveState=active
SubState=running

managed handoff log:
[2026-06-04T18:06:36.961Z] starting managed update command: openclaw update --yes --channel beta --timeout 2700
[2026-06-04T18:06:36.963Z] managed update command pid=2744165
{
  "status": "ok",
  "mode": "npm",
  "root": "<proof-root>/npm-prefix/lib/node_modules/openclaw",
  "before": { "version": "2026.6.1" },
  "after": { "version": "2026.6.2-beta.1" },
  "steps": [
    {
      "name": "global update",
      "command": "npm i -g --prefix <proof-root>/npm-prefix/lib/node_modules/.openclaw-update-stage-... openclaw@beta --no-fund --no-audit --loglevel=error --min-release-age=0",
      "exitCode": 0
    },
    {
      "name": "global install swap",
      "command": "swap <proof-root>/npm-prefix/lib/node_modules/.openclaw-update-stage-.../lib/node_modules/openclaw -> <proof-root>/npm-prefix/lib/node_modules/openclaw",
      "exitCode": 0
    },
    {
      "name": "openclaw doctor",
      "command": "/usr/bin/node <proof-root>/npm-prefix/lib/node_modules/openclaw/dist/index.js doctor --non-interactive --fix",
      "exitCode": 0
    }
  ],
  "durationMs": 19607
}
[2026-06-04T18:07:09.355Z] managed update command exited code=0 signal=null
```

Gateway health after the package update and service restart:

```json
{
  "ok": true,
  "plugins": {
    "loaded": [
      "browser",
      "canvas",
      "device-pair",
      "file-transfer",
      "memory-core",
      "phone-control",
      "talk-voice"
    ],
    "errors": []
  },
  "sessions": {
    "path": "<proof-root>/state/agents/main/sessions/sessions.json",
    "count": 0,
    "recent": []
  }
}
```

Observed result after fix: a packaged user-systemd gateway started from this PR handed startup auto-update to the detached managed-service updater, ran the real `openclaw update --yes --channel beta --json` path through npm, swapped the package root, ran doctor successfully, restarted the gateway service, and returned healthy.

What was not tested: macOS launchd and Windows Scheduled Task proofs were not run. This proof covers the packaged Linux user-systemd path that ClawSweeper asked for; the existing focused tests still cover the helper-level channel, timeout, marker-stripping, and restart-continuation behavior.

Before evidence (optional but encouraged): ```
✓ without this patch, the gateway process printed an undeliverable 'gateway service restarted by auto-update' wall message and the supervisor saw a no-op restart that could stall further commands
```

## Tests and validation

```bash
pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test:fast  # all passing
```

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`) Yes — unattended auto-update no longer produces an undeliverable restart continuation message, and the gateway process no longer runs the update command as a direct child with inherited service markers.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`) No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`) No.

What is the highest-risk area? The managed-service handoff path runs inside the live gateway service. If the handoff command fails or blocks, the gateway could stall or enter an inconsistent startup state.

How is that risk mitigated? The handoff uses the existing managed-service `spawnManagedService` helper with a timeout, same as `update.run`. The timeout prevents indefinite blocking, and the CLI package-update guard still applies on the handoff target.

## Current review state

Next action: maintainer review.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof? Maintainer review.

target.title: perf(agents): cache provider tool schema normalization
target.body:
## Summary
- Cache provider-normalized tool schema `parameters` for repeated embedded agent turns when the active provider hook exposes a stable hook-owned cache key.
- Keep the cache conservative: the key includes provider hook identity plus provider/model/runtime inputs and ordered original tool schema signatures; cached values clone only tool `parameters`, not tool objects, execute closures, approval state, or guards.
- Add `OPENCLAW_TOOL_SCHEMA_CACHE=0` as a kill switch, SDK docs/API surface for `resolveToolSchemaCacheKey`, and focused cache-key, clone-safety, env-disable, eviction, and runtime-handle coverage.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: repeated embedded agent turns no longer redo provider tool-schema normalization when the provider hook identity, provider/model/config inputs, and ordered original tool schema signature are unchanged. Fresh tool objects, execute closures, approval state, and runtime guards are still rebuilt per turn.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on macOS, branch `tool-schema-cache-pr3`, current PR head `0a027a65316eddb2d30c99bbe602295775da6ea5`. The runtime proof directly invoked the current `normalizeProviderToolSchemas` production entry with a provider runtime handle that implements `normalizeToolSchemas` and `resolveToolSchemaCacheKey`, then verified cache behavior and the env kill switch from terminal output.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: At current head, ran a `node --import tsx --input-type=module` terminal proof that reset the provider tool schema cache, called `normalizeProviderToolSchemas` twice with the same provider/model/api/schema signature but different `execute` closures, then called it once with `OPENCLAW_TOOL_SCHEMA_CACHE=0`. Also ran the focused Plugin SDK API baseline check and provider/tool-schema test shard.

Evidence after fix:

```text
$ git rev-parse HEAD
0a027a65316eddb2d30c99bbe602295775da6ea5

$ node --import tsx --input-type=module <<'SCRIPT'
...current-head runtime proof script invoking normalizeProviderToolSchemas...
SCRIPT
{
  "head": "0a027a65316eddb2d30c99bbe602295775da6ea5",
  "normalizeCalls": 2,
  "cacheStatsAfterFirstNormalize": {
    "bypass": 0,
    "hit": 0,
    "miss": 1,
    "store": 1,
    "size": 1
  },
  "cacheStatsAfterSecondNormalize": {
    "bypass": 0,
    "hit": 1,
    "miss": 1,
    "store": 1,
    "size": 1
  },
  "cacheStatsAfterDisabledNormalize": {
    "bypass": 1,
    "hit": 1,
    "miss": 1,
    "store": 1,
    "size": 1
  },
  "normalizedParametersEqual": true,
  "secondRunExecuteClosurePreserved": true,
  "disabledRunExecuteClosurePreserved": true,
  "cacheHitIncreasedOnSecondNormalize": true,
  "normalizeHookNotCalledOnSecondRun": true,
  "disabledBypassIncreased": true
}

$ node --max-old-space-size=8192 --import tsx scripts/generate-plugin-sdk-api-baseline.ts --check
OK docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/tool-schema-runtime.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-tools.test.ts src/plugins/provider-runtime.test.ts --reporter=dot
[test] passed 3 Vitest shards in 8.46s
```

Observed result after fix: the first current-head normalization produced one cache miss and one store. The second normalization with the same provider/model/api/schema signature produced one cache hit, did not call the provider normalization hook again, and still preserved the fresh second tool object's `execute` closure. With `OPENCLAW_TOOL_SCHEMA_CACHE=0`, the next call bypassed the cache and preserved that call's fresh `execute` closure.

What was not tested: full live-provider latency and a remote LLM turn were not tested in this current-head refresh. This PR caches only provider-normalized schema `parameters`; it does not cache complete tool inventory/materialization, policy resolution, prompt inputs, or full agent-turn startup.

## Summary

- Problem: The Deep Sleep phase writes per-phase report files (`memory/dreaming/deep/YYYY-MM-DD.md`) but never writes the documented `## Deep Sleep` summary section to `DREAMS.md`. Per docs: "Deep phase writes a `## Deep Sleep` summary into `DREAMS.md` and optionally writes `memory/dreaming/deep/YYYY-MM-DD.md`."
- What changed: `writeDeepDreamingReport` now always writes the `## Deep Sleep` summary to `DREAMS.md`. When an existing `## Deep Sleep` section is present, it is replaced in-place (including when the heading appears at file position 0 without a leading newline). Other sections in DREAMS.md are preserved. The separate per-phase report file is still conditionally written when storage mode requests it. Added test coverage for separateReports=false (DREAMS.md only) and section replacement (non-destructive, no duplicates).

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Linked Issues

- Closes #91051

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Deep Sleep phase never writes `## Deep Sleep` to DREAMS.md (#91051).

**Real environment tested:** Node v22.22.0, Linux x86_64.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=4 pnpm test -- extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-markdown.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-phases.test.ts

target.title: fix(cli): bound native hook relay process lifetime (#90993)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #90993 by bounding native hook relay CLI (`openclaw-hooks`) process lifetime so leaked subprocesses cannot accumulate when harnesses leave stdin open or gateway handles remain referenced.

## Root cause

Three stacked issues in the relay CLI path:

1. **Unbounded stdin read** — `readStreamText()` blocked forever on open stdin before `--timeout` applied.
2. **No hard exit** — relay action set `process.exitCode` only; Node stayed alive while stdin/WebSocket handles remained open.
3. **Narrow timeout scope** — `--timeout` bounded only bridge/gateway RPC, not stdin or overall process lifetime.

## Changes

- `src/cli/native-hook-relay-cli.ts`: time-bound stdin read; await stdout/stderr writes; flush streams before forced exit; export deadline + exit helpers.
- `src/cli/hooks-cli.ts`: hard process deadline (`exit 124` on overrun); `exitNativeHookRelayProcess()` after relay work so Codex receives complete deny JSON.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: Native hook relay CLI (`openclaw-hooks`) subprocesses leaked when stdin stayed open past `--timeout`; processes accumulated until host OOM (#90993).
- Real environment tested: Local Linux checkout, branch `fix/native-hook-relay-process-exit`, Node 22+, `pnpm build` completed on fix branch; before repro on `main`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
pnpm build
for i in $(seq 1 10); do
  tail -f /dev/null | node scripts/run-node.mjs hooks relay \
    --provider codex --relay-id "mre-after-$i" --generation "mre-gen-$i" \
    --event pre_tool_use --timeout 5000 &
  sleep 2
done
sleep 12
ps -eo pid,comm,rss | grep openclaw-hooks || echo "(no openclaw-hooks — expected)"
```

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

**Before fix (main):** 10 `openclaw-hooks` processes, ~250–450 MB RSS each (~2.5 GB total), alive past `--timeout 5000`.

![before-fix](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0494e803-205c-44cb-8dd6-b8d493f12877)

**After fix (this branch):** 0 `openclaw-hooks` after deadline; terminal shows `(no openclaw-hooks — expected)`.

![after-fix](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/613b4bf5-69a8-45b1-87c5-15564892b1c5)

```text
$ ps -eo pid,comm,rss | grep openclaw-hooks || echo "(no openclaw-hooks — expected)"
(no openclaw-hooks — expected)
```

- Observed result after fix: Open-stdin relay exits within stdin timeout and hard process deadline; no lingering `openclaw-hooks` subprocesses after `sleep 12`.
- What was not tested: Multi-hour gateway + heartbeat + Codex production soak; full Codex harness with live gateway registration.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: Reproduced via open-stdin MRE (`tail -f /dev/null` pipe), not full production Codex heartbeat loop.

## Tests and validation

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/native-hook-relay-cli.test.ts
pnpm check
pnpm check:test-types
pnpm build
```

- 19/19 tests in `native-hook-relay-cli.test.ts` (stdin timeout, flush-before-exit, async stdout deny JSON)
- `pnpm check` and `pnpm check:test-types` pass locally

## Release note (for maintainers; no CHANGELOG edit per CONTRIBUTING)

CLI: bound native hook relay stdin read, flush hook output before exit, and force process exit so `openclaw-hooks` subprocesses cannot leak after hook events. (#90993)

## Sign-off

- Models used: AI-assisted
- Submitter effort: implemented fix + tests + local MRE verification

target.title: fix(daemon): strip schtasks backslash prefix when matching gateway task name
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem**: On Windows, the live `\OpenClaw Gateway` Scheduled Task is falsely flagged as an extra gateway-like service with a `schtasks /Delete` cleanup hint. Following the hint removes the user's auto-start mechanism. Issue #90494.
- **Root Cause**: `schtasks /Query /FO LIST /V` returns task names with a leading `\` (e.g. `TaskName:\OpenClaw Gateway`). `isOpenClawGatewayTaskName()` compares the raw name against `startsWith("openclaw gateway")` — fails because `\` comes first.
- **Fix**: Strip leading backslash before comparison: `normalized.replace(/^\\+/, "")`.
- **What changed**: `src/daemon/inspect.ts` (+6/-1) and `src/daemon/inspect.test.ts` (+4/-1).

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: `\OpenClaw Gateway` Scheduled Task falsely flagged as extra gateway service with `schtasks /Delete` cleanup recommendation.
- **Real environment tested**: Windows 11 Home China x64 (10.0.22631), Node 24.16.0. Two real Scheduled Tasks (`\OpenClaw Gateway` canonical, `\Clawdbot Legacy` legacy) created via `schtasks /Create`. Full `schtasks /Query /FO LIST /V` captured (257 tasks).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Parsed all 257 real tasks from `schtasks /Query /FO LIST /V` via PowerShell, applied before/after detection logic to each task. Console output captured below.
- **Evidence after fix**: Real console output from parsing 257 Windows Scheduled Tasks with before/after detection logic:

```
Total tasks parsed: 257

Task name: "\Clawdbot Legacy"
  Normalized:   "\clawdbot legacy"
  Stripped :    "clawdbot legacy"
  BEFORE fix: NOT SKIPPED -> correctly flagged as legacy extra service
  AFTER fix : NOT SKIPPED -> correctly flagged as legacy extra service

Task name: "\OpenClaw Gateway"
  Normalized:   "\openclaw gateway"
  Stripped :    "openclaw gateway"
  BEFORE fix: NOT SKIPPED -> flagged as extra service -> schtasks /Delete hint shown
  AFTER fix : SKIPPED (recognized as canonical gateway launcher)
```

- **Observed result after fix**: `\OpenClaw Gateway` is correctly identified as the canonical task and excluded from the extra-services warning. `\Clawdbot Legacy` is still correctly detected as a legacy extra service. No regression for the other 255 system tasks.
- **What was not tested**: Running a full live gateway process launched by the Scheduled Task. The fix is a single string-sanitization in a 7-line identity matcher; the before/after contract is fully verifiable from the real schtasks parse output above.

Fixes #90494

commands.author: zenglingbiao

target.title: fix(memory-core): write Deep Sleep summary into DREAMS.md after deep dreaming phase (fixes #91051)
target.body:
**Summary**: The deep dreaming phase writes its structured report to `memory/dreaming/deep/YYYY-MM-DD.md` but never updates the `## Deep Sleep` section in `DREAMS.md` as documented.

**Root Cause**: `writeDeepDreamingReport()` in `dreaming-markdown.ts` only writes to the separate report file under `memory/dreaming/deep/`. There is no corresponding write to `DREAMS.md` for the `## Deep Sleep` summary block. Unlike the light and REM phases that write managed blocks to `memory/YYYY-MM-DD.md`, the deep phase had no path to populate `DREAMS.md` with its structured summary (ranked/promoted counts).

**Fix**: Added `writeDeepDreamingToDreamsMd()` in `dreaming-markdown.ts` that writes a `## Deep Sleep` managed block (with `<!-- openclaw:dreaming:deep:start -->` / `<!-- openclaw:dreaming:deep:end -->` markers) into `DREAMS.md`. Called from the deep dreaming sweep in `dreaming.ts` immediately after `writeDeepDreamingReport()`.

**Verification**:
- `pnpm build` — passes
- `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-markdown.test.ts` — 5 tests pass
- `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming.test.ts` — 50 tests pass
- `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-narrative.test.ts` — 96 tests pass

## Real Behavior Proof

**Behavior or issue addressed**: After a deep dreaming phase sweep, `DREAMS.md` now receives a `## Deep Sleep` section summarizing ranked/promoted counts, matching the documented behavior.

**Real environment tested**:
- OS: Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64
- Node: v22.22.0
- OpenClaw: 2026.6.1 (66b91d78feb33d62e2f82ae1d8689c48519f5530)

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
1. `pnpm build` — builds cleanly
2. `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-markdown.test.ts` — all tests pass
3. `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming.test.ts` — all 50 tests pass

**Evidence after fix**: Verified with real file I/O in an isolated temp directory. The function correctly writes a `## Deep Sleep` managed block into `DREAMS.md`:

Fresh workspace (no existing DREAMS.md):
```
## Deep Sleep
<!-- openclaw:dreaming:deep:start -->
- Ranked 5 candidate(s).
- Promoted 2 candidate(s).
<!-- openclaw:dreaming:deep:end -->
```

Idempotent update (subsequent run replaces the block):
```
## Deep Sleep
<!-- openclaw:dreaming:deep:start -->
- Ranked 7 candidate(s).
- Promoted 4 candidate(s).
<!-- openclaw:dreaming:deep:end -->
```

Existing DREAMS.md with other content — the managed block is appended without disturbing existing content:
```
# Dream Diary

Some existing content

## Deep Sleep
<!-- openclaw:dreaming:deep:start -->
- Repaired recall artifacts: 0 stale entries.
- Ranked 8 candidate(s) for durable promotion.
- Promoted 3 candidate(s) into MEMORY.md.
<!-- openclaw:dreaming:deep:end -->
```

Source: [extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-markdown.ts](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/blob/main/extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-markdown.ts)

**Observed result after fix**: Three scenarios tested with real file I/O in isolated temp directories — all produce correct `## Deep Sleep` managed blocks. `pnpm test` for all memory-core dreaming tests exits 0 (151 tests pass across 3 test files). The function correctly handles fresh workspace, idempotent update, and existing DREAMS.md with unrelated content.

**What was not tested**: End-to-end cron-driven deep dreaming sweep with a live gateway was not tested in this environment.

**Regression Test Plan**:
- `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-markdown.test.ts`
- `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming.test.ts`
- `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-narrative.test.ts`
- `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/dreaming-phases.test.ts`

**Review Findings Addressed**: N/A (new PR)

**Merge Risk**: Low. The change adds a new function call immediately after an existing write; it does not modify any existing behavior. The new function follows the same managed block pattern already used throughout memory-core (`replaceManagedMarkdownBlock`). If DREAMS.md does not exist, it is created. The managed block is scoped to `## Deep Sleep` markers and will not interfere with the diary section managed by the narrative code.

target.title: fix(agents): prevent ReDoS in background-session name derivation
target.body:
## Summary

- `tokenizeCommand` (`src/agents/bash-tools.shared.ts`) splits a shell command into tokens with the regex `/(?:[^\s"']+|"(?:\\.|[^"])*"|'(?:\\.|[^'])*')+/g`. The quoted-span alternatives `"(?:\\.|[^"])*"` / `'(?:\\.|[^'])*'` are ambiguous: a backslash matches **both** `\\.` and `[^"]`, so a quoted span that never closes forces the engine to try every partition of the backslash run. Runtime is exponential in the number of backslashes after an unterminated quote - classic catastrophic backtracking.
- Reach: the tokenizer feeds `deriveSessionName(command)`, which runs on `session.command` whenever background bash sessions are listed, labeled, or sent keys (`src/agents/bash-tools.process.ts` x10, `bash-process-references.ts`, `bash-tools.process-send-keys.ts`). `session.command` is the agent-issued background command string, so a single crafted background command (e.g. via prompt-injected web/repo/inbound content) pins the Node event loop and stalls the whole agent/gateway process - same impact class as the previously fixed interpreter-heuristic hang (#61881).
- Fix: exclude the backslash from the negated character classes (`[^"]` -> `[^"\\]`, `[^']` -> `[^'\\]`) so a backslash only ever matches the `\\.` escape alternative. This removes the ambiguity and makes matching linear. It is exactly the form the sibling tokenizers already use (`src/agents/sessions/model-registry.ts:219-220`, `src/agents/tool-display-common.ts:472`); `tokenizeCommand` was the lone instance still on the unsafe pattern. Well-formed and escaped quoted spans tokenize identically to before.

## Tests

- `src/agents/bash-tools.shared.test.ts`: added a `deriveSessionName` block - well-formed quoted commands still label as before, backslash-bearing quoted spans still group into one token, and an unterminated quote followed by 50,000 backslashes returns a string in well under 100ms (times out / hangs on the old regex). Existing `resolveSandboxWorkdir` / `readEnvInt` cases unchanged and green.
- Ran the touched file with the repo runner: 10/10 pass. Type-aware oxlint and oxfmt clean on the touched files.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior addressed: a background bash command whose stored `session.command` contains an unterminated quote followed by a run of backslashes triggers catastrophic regex backtracking in `tokenizeCommand` via `deriveSessionName`, hanging the Node event loop (denial of service) every time the session list / labels / send-keys path derives a name. `session.command` is agent-controlled, so injected content can reach it.
- Real environment tested: Linux, Node 22.19, exercising the actual production `deriveSessionName` exported from `src/agents/bash-tools.shared.ts` through the repo Vitest module loader (no copied regex). The before column ran the unmodified `origin/main` source of that file; the after column ran the patched file; everything else identical.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: called `deriveSessionName` on a benign command and on `node "` + `"\\".repeat(n)` for n = 22,26,28,30,32,34, timing each with `process.hrtime.bigint()`, over current head `c6829198088` with the patch, and (for the before column) over the `origin/main` source of the same file.
- Evidence after fix:

  ```
  === deriveSessionName timing (AFTER fix, head c6829198088 + patch) ===
  benign  "node server.js --port 8080" : 0.56 ms
  malicious unterminated quote + 22 backslashes : 0.03 ms
  malicious unterminated quote + 26 backslashes : 0.01 ms
  malicious unterminated quote + 28 backslashes : 0.01 ms
  malicious unterminated quote + 30 backslashes : 0.02 ms
  malicious unterminated quote + 32 backslashes : 0.01 ms
  malicious unterminated quote + 34 backslashes : 0.01 ms
  ```

- Observed result after fix: timing is flat (~0.01ms) across the malicious inputs and benign labeling is unchanged; the event loop is no longer blocked.
- What was not tested: a fully wired gateway round-trip that lists a malicious background session through a live channel (the proof drives the exact function that runs on `session.command` rather than issuing a live agent run); the sibling tokenizers in `model-registry.ts` / `tool-display-common.ts`, which already use the safe form and are unchanged.
- Before evidence: the same script over the unpatched `origin/main` source of `bash-tools.shared.ts`:

  ```
  === deriveSessionName timing (BEFORE fix, origin/main c6829198088) ===
  benign  "node server.js --port 8080" : 0.59 ms
  malicious unterminated quote + 22 backslashes :   0.71 ms
  malicious unterminated quote + 26 backslashes :   5.01 ms
  malicious unterminated quote + 28 backslashes :  11.57 ms
  malicious unterminated quote + 30 backslashes :  28.42 ms
  malicious unterminated quote + 32 backslashes :  70.98 ms
  malicious unterminated quote + 34 backslashes : 163.79 ms
  ```

  Time roughly doubles per +2 backslashes, so n in the 40s reaches seconds and n in the 50s reaches minutes of fully-blocked event loop.

target.title: fix(feishu): use union_id as fallback for @mention detection in multi-bot groups
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #40768

When multiple Feishu bots share the same group, @mentioning a specific bot is not recognized. The bot logs `message in group ... did not mention bot` even though it's correctly mentioned.

## Root Cause

Feishu `open_id` is **app-scoped** — the same bot has a different `open_id` depending on which app's token is used to query it. Confirmed by querying the same message with two different bot tokens:

```
# Same @mention, different app contexts:
writer token → mention open_id: ou_4ede388c79638a9057a432c3e3832f91
tron token   → mention open_id: ou_04dc13327d491d9e36da57826a4131f0
```

WebSocket push events deliver mention `open_id`s in a specific app context that may not match the receiving bot's own `open_id`, causing `checkBotMentioned()` to always return `false`.

## Fix

`union_id` is globally unique across all apps for the same entity — it doesn't vary by app context. This PR:

1. Fetches `union_id` alongside `open_id` from `bot/v3/info` at startup
2. Stores it in a new `botUnionIds` map in monitor state
3. Passes it through to `checkBotMentioned()` as a fallback
4. Matches mentions by `union_id` when `open_id` doesn't match

The `open_id` match is preserved as the primary check; `union_id` is a fallback, so existing behavior is unchanged for single-bot setups.

## Files Changed

- `types.ts`: add `botUnionId` to `FeishuProbeResult`
- `probe.ts`: extract `union_id` from `bot/v3/info` response
- `monitor.startup.ts`: propagate `botUnionId` through `FeishuMonitorBotIdentity`
- `monitor.state.ts`: add `botUnionIds` map with proper clear/delete
- `monitor.account.ts`: store/clear `botUnionId`, pass to `handleFeishuMessage`
- `bot.ts`: accept `botUnionId` in `checkBotMentioned` and `parseFeishuMessageEvent`, match by `union_id` when `open_id` mismatches

target.title: [AI-assisted] Persist agent-run error replies in chat history
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: A started Control UI agent run could surface a terminal provider/runtime error live, but `chat.history` after refresh did not retain an assistant error turn for that failed run.
- Solution: Treat agent-run failure replies as real error payloads and make Gateway persist exactly one display-safe assistant error transcript entry for returned agent errors, even when the runtime emitted only an internal error stub.
- What changed: Runner failure payloads are marked `isError`, Gateway appends a deduped `<runId>:assistant-error` transcript entry for returned agent errors, and regression coverage now locks the returned-error, runtime-stub, and duplicate/idempotency paths.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): No provider behavior changes, no delivery routing changes, no config/lifecycle changes, no raw transcript writes, and no dependency or lockfile changes.

## Motivation

- A user should not lose the visible assistant error after refreshing the Control UI. If a started run fails before normal assistant output, the live error event and durable history should agree.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #89197
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: A real Gateway/WebSocket Control UI run that fails at provider transport now leaves exactly one assistant error in refreshed `chat.history`.
- Real environment tested: Disposable Linux OpenClaw checkout with the patched PR branch, real Gateway server startup, real WebSocket RPC client, channels skipped with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`, and an intentionally unreachable OpenAI-compatible base URL to trigger the provider failure without secrets.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts -- -t 'assistant error|returned agent-run error payloads|runtime emitted an internal error stub'
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.error-broadcast.test.ts src/agents/session-tool-result-guard.test.ts
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/followup-runner.test.ts -- -t 'Agent failed before reply|assistant error|fallback candidate|error stub'
OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1 node --import tsx ../real-chat-history-proof.ts after-gateway-force-v3
/usr/bin/time -v node_modules/.bin/tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json
```

- Evidence after fix:

```text
Real runtime proof:
- provider transport failed with fetch failed / Connection error
- agent.wait status: error
- immediateHistoryCount: 3
- refreshHistoryCount: 3
- immediateAssistantCount: 1
- refreshAssistantCount: 1
- chatErrorEventCount: 1
- result: ONE_ASSISTANT_ERROR_IN_REFRESHED_HISTORY

Focused Gateway proof: 3 passed, 102 skipped.
Adjacent Gateway proof: 107 passed.
Session tool result guard proof: 35 passed.
Runner/Followup focused proof: 8 passed, 214 skipped.
git diff --check: clean.
```

- Observed result after fix: The live provider failure still broadcasts the expected chat error, and both immediate and post-reconnect `chat.history` contain one assistant error text: `Agent failed before reply: LLM request failed: network connection error.`
- What was not tested: Full repository-wide test suite, successful `tsgo` core on this testserver, real provider credentials, and a browser screenshot.
- Before evidence:

```text
Unpatched main proof for the same real Gateway/WebSocket/provider-failure path:
- sendStatus: started
- agent.wait status: ok
- immediateHistoryCount: 2
- refreshHistoryCount: 2
- immediateAssistantCount: 0
- refreshAssistantCount: 0
- chatErrorEventCount: 0
- result: ASSISTANT_ERROR_HISTORY_MISMATCH
```

## Root Cause

- Root cause: Runner final failure replies were not consistently marked as error payloads, and Gateway treated the runtime assistant-error callback as if it guaranteed a Control UI-visible `chat.history` assistant turn.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Previous coverage asserted live error broadcast behavior but did not prove refreshed `chat.history` retained an assistant error after a started real runtime failure.
- Contributing context (if known): Runtime/session guard error stubs can be internal transcript-only markers; Gateway still needs to durably mirror returned terminal agent errors into the Control UI history path.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [x] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts`, and the temporary real Gateway/WebSocket proof harness.
- Scenario the test should lock in: A started run returns an agent error; Gateway appends one assistant error transcript entry, keeps the live error terminal event, survives reconnect/history refresh, and does not duplicate the assistant error.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The source tests cover the exact Gateway and runner seams, while the real proof exercises server startup, WebSocket RPC, provider transport failure, `agent.wait`, and refreshed `chat.history` in one flow.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Existing broadcast tests covered visible error delivery but not durable assistant transcript history.
- If no new test is added, why not: New regression assertions were added.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Control UI users should now see the same failed assistant turn after refresh that they saw live when a started agent run fails before normal assistant output.

## Diagram

```text
real provider/runtime failure
  -> runner returns final failure payload marked isError
  -> Gateway returnedAgentErrorPayloads branch
  -> append one sanitized assistant error transcript entry with <runId>:assistant-error
  -> broadcast existing chat error terminal event
  -> reconnect chat.history still includes one assistant error
```

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No.
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No.
- New/changed network calls? No.
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No.
- Data access scope changed? No new scope; the change writes a sanitized assistant error to the same session transcript path already used for Gateway assistant persistence.
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Not applicable.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux disposable test checkout
- Runtime/container: OpenClaw repository scripts with installed dependencies
- Model/provider: OpenAI-compatible provider configured to an unreachable local base URL for deterministic transport failure; no real credentials used
- Integration/channel (if any): Control UI-style Gateway WebSocket RPC; external channels skipped with `OPENCLAW_SKIP_CHANNELS=1`
- Relevant config (redacted): Dummy redacted API key, local unreachable base URL, temporary state directory

### Steps

1. Start the real Gateway server from the patched checkout.
2. Connect a WebSocket client and send `chat.send` for a Control UI session.
3. Trigger a deterministic provider transport failure through the unreachable model base URL.
4. Wait for the run with `agent.wait`.
5. Read `chat.history` immediately.
6. Reconnect a fresh WebSocket client and read `chat.history` again.
7. Count assistant text messages and chat error events.

### Expected

- The run surfaces a live terminal error.
- Refreshed `chat.history` contains exactly one assistant error message for the failed run.
- The assistant error is not duplicated.

### Actual

- Before: refreshed `chat.history` had zero assistant messages for the failed run.
- After: refreshed `chat.history` had exactly one assistant error message and one chat error event.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

```text
Before real proof result: ASSISTANT_ERROR_HISTORY_MISMATCH
After real proof result: ONE_ASSISTANT_ERROR_IN_REFRESHED_HISTORY
After source proof: Gateway focused 3/3, Gateway adjacent 107/107, session guard 35/35, Runner/Followup focused 8/8.
TypeScript core check on the testserver did not produce diagnostics; the process was terminated by signal 9 at about 1.78 GB RSS, so this is reported as a testserver resource blocker rather than a green typecheck claim.
```

## Human Verification

target.title: fix: split image setup and request timeout semantics
target.body:
## Summary
- split image-description timeout handling so setup and provider request both use the configured `tools.media.image.timeoutSeconds` budget instead of shrinking the request budget by elapsed setup time
- preserve the full configured timeout for the provider request instead of silently subtracting setup latency down to a tiny remaining budget
- add focused tests covering request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and slow-setup request behavior
- keep public docs user-facing: the image timeout still applies to the request itself and is not reduced by earlier preparation work
- keep the heavier image-tool latency and runtime-loading reductions for follow-up PRs after this timeout-semantics fix

## Verification
- `git diff --check`
- `env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --provider docker --docker-image node:22-bookworm --timing-json --shell -- "bash -lc 'corepack enable >/dev/null 2>&1 && env CI=1 NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=900000 corepack pnpm test src/media-understanding/image.test.ts'"`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: image timeout errors no longer collapse setup latency into a tiny remaining provider timeout; setup and request failures now report distinct phases.
Real environment tested: Crabbox `docker` provider with `node:22-bookworm`, synced from this worktree.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: run the targeted media-understanding Vitest file via Crabbox Docker using the command above.
Evidence after fix: `src/media-understanding/image.test.ts` passes with 25 tests; assertions cover request timeout messaging, setup timeout messaging, and a slow setup that still passes the full configured timeout through to the provider request.
Observed result after fix: the test suite passed, provider-request calls still received `timeoutMs: 1000` after a mocked `400ms` setup delay, and timeout messages now distinguish `setup` vs `request`.
What was not tested: live provider behavior and the follow-up PRs that will reduce the image runtime-loading latency itself.

target.title: [codex] Fix Anthropic Vertex npm audit regression
target.body:
## Summary

- remove the `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk` runtime dependency from the shipped OpenClaw package
- replace it with a small local Anthropic Vertex client built on the existing `@anthropic-ai/sdk` and `google-auth-library@10.6.2`
- update the lockfile so the npm package no longer pulls `google-auth-library@9 -> gaxios@6 -> uuid@9`

## Why

`openclaw@2026.5.2-beta.1` introduced a clean npm audit regression for consumers: `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk@0.16.0` depends on `google-auth-library@^9.4.2`, which resolves to `gaxios@6.7.1` and `uuid@9.0.1`. npm ignores dependency package `overrides`, so the published OpenClaw package still audited with two moderate findings even though the pnpm workspace override resolved `uuid` internally.

## Validation

- `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
- `pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic-vertex`
- `pnpm exec oxlint extensions/anthropic-vertex/stream-runtime.ts`
- `pnpm audit --prod --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- packed this branch with `npm pack`, installed the tarball into a fresh npm project, and verified `npm audit --omit=dev --json` reports 0 vulnerabilities
- tarball install dependency tree no longer contains `@anthropic-ai/vertex-sdk`, `google-auth-library@9`, `gaxios@6`, or `uuid@9`

target.title: fix(commitments): stop hardcoding fastMode in extraction (#78451)
target.body:
## Summary

MiniMax Coding Plan operators whose plan owns `MiniMax-M2.7` but not the `-highspeed` variant hit `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` on every commitment extraction pass, because the commitments extraction path passed `fastMode: true` unconditionally to `runEmbeddedPiAgent`, which caused the MiniMax stream wrapper's `createMinimaxFastModeWrapper` to rewrite the base model id to `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`.

## Root Cause

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts:242` (pre-fix): the call to `runEmbeddedPiAgent` for hidden commitment extraction hardcoded `fastMode: true`.
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/minimax-stream-wrappers.ts:24`: the MiniMax stream wrapper accepts a `fastMode` boolean and, when true, swaps the base model id to its `-highspeed` variant.
- Result: every commitments extraction request silently flipped `MiniMax-M2.7` → `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed`, which Coding Plan operators do not own. MiniMax responds with HTTP 500 / error code 2061. Reporter verified the Coding Plan owns the base model but not highspeed.
- The reporter listed three valid fix shapes (drop the hardcode, add a config field, or inherit agent fastMode). This PR uses option 1: dropping the hardcode lets the existing `resolveFastModeState` helper continue to control fast-mode for the rest of the agent runtime, and commitments extraction inherits the same default-false behavior.

## Changes

- `src/commitments/runtime.ts`: remove `fastMode: true` from the `runEmbeddedPiAgent` payload built in the commitments extraction path. Add a comment explaining the reason so this does not regress.
- `src/commitments/runtime.test.ts`: extend the existing `runEmbeddedPiAgentMock` assertion to assert `fastMode` is **not** in the payload, locking in the regression fix.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: single-line entry under Unreleased / Fixes.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: On every commitment extraction pass, MiniMax Coding Plan operators receive `HTTP 500: your current token plan not support model, MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed (2061)` from the MiniMax server because the agent runtime requested `MiniMax-M2.7-highspeed` even though their plan only owns `MiniMax-M2.7`. The reporter (`@advaitpaliwal`) verified `commitments.enabled: true, commitments.maxPerDay: 10` config, then ran a real Coding Plan session, observed extraction always selecting the highspeed variant, and traced the root cause to `dist/runtime-CXt7BFiH.js` line 386 (`fastMode: true` hardcoded).
